payload.msg: â€œThat is doubtful,â€ said Prince Andrew. â€œMonsieur le Vicomte quite rightly supposes that matters have already gone too far. I think it will be difficult to return to the old regime.â€
â€œFrom what I have heard,â€ said Pierre, blushing and breaking into the conversation, â€œalmost all the aristocracy has already gone over to Bonaparteâ€™s side.â€
â€œIt is the Buonapartists who say that,â€ replied the vicomte without looking at Pierre. â€œAt the present time it is difficult to know the real state of French public opinion.â€
â€œBonaparte has said so,â€ remarked Prince Andrew with a sarcastic smile.
It was evident that he did not like the vicomte and was aiming his remarks at him, though without looking at him.

â€œâ€˜I showed them the path to glory, but they did not follow it,â€™â€ Prince Andrew continued after a short silence, again quoting Napoleonâ€™s words. â€œâ€˜I opened my antechambers and they crowded in.â€™ I do not know how far he was justified in saying so.â€

â€œNot in the least,â€ replied the vicomte. â€œAfter the murder of the duc even the most partial ceased to regard him as a hero. If to some people,â€ he went on, turning to Anna PÃ¡vlovna, â€œhe ever was a hero, after the murder of the duc there was one martyr more in heaven and one hero less on earth.â€

Before Anna PÃ¡vlovna and the others had time to smile their appreciation of the vicomteâ€™s epigram, Pierre again broke into the conversation, and though Anna PÃ¡vlovna felt sure he would say something inappropriate, she was unable to stop him.

â€œThe execution of the Duc dâ€™Enghien,â€ declared Monsieur Pierre, â€œwas a political necessity, and it seems to me that Napoleon showed greatness of soul by not fearing to take on himself the whole responsibility of that deed.â€
â€œDieu! Mon Dieu!â€ muttered Anna PÃ¡vlovna in a terrified whisper.
â€œWhat, Monsieur Pierre... Do you consider that assassination shows greatness of soul?â€ said the little princess, smiling and drawing her work nearer to her.
â€œCapital!â€ said Prince Hippolyte in English, and began slapping his knee with the palm of his hand.
The vicomte merely shrugged his shoulders. Pierre looked solemnly at his audience over his spectacles and continued.

â€œI say so,â€ he continued desperately, â€œbecause the Bourbons fled from the Revolution leaving the people to anarchy, and Napoleon alone understood the Revolution and quelled it, and so for the general good, he could not stop short for the sake of one manâ€™s life.â€
â€œWonâ€™t you come over to the other table?â€ suggested Anna PÃ¡vlovna.
But Pierre continued his speech without heeding her.

â€œNo,â€ cried he, becoming more and more eager, â€œNapoleon is great because he rose superior to the Revolution, suppressed its abuses, preserved all that was good in itâ€”equality of citizenship and freedom of speech and of the pressâ€”and only for that reason did he obtain power.â€
â€œYes, if having obtained power, without availing himself of it to commit murder he had restored it to the rightful king, I should have called him a great man,â€ remarked the vicomte.

â€œHe could not do that. The people only gave him power that he might rid them of the Bourbons and because they saw that he was a great man. The Revolution was a grand thing!â€ continued Monsieur Pierre, betraying by this desperate and provocative proposition his extreme youth and his wish to express all that was in his mind.
â€œWhat? Revolution and regicide a grand thing?... Well, after that... But wonâ€™t you come to this other table?â€ repeated Anna PÃ¡vlovna.
â€œRousseauâ€™s Contrat Social,â€ said the vicomte with a tolerant smile.
â€œI am not speaking of regicide, I am speaking about ideas.â€
â€œYes: ideas of robbery, murder, and regicide,â€ again interjected an ironical voice.

â€œThose were extremes, no doubt, but they are not what is most important. What is important are the rights of man, emancipation from prejudices, and equality of citizenship, and all these ideas Napoleon has retained in full force.â€

â€œLiberty and equality,â€ said the vicomte contemptuously, as if at last deciding seriously to prove to this youth how foolish his words were, â€œhigh-sounding words which have long been discredited. Who does not love liberty and equality? Even our Saviour preached liberty and equality. Have people since the Revolution become happier? On the contrary. We wanted liberty, but Buonaparte has destroyed it.â€

Prince Andrew kept looking with an amused smile from Pierre to the vicomte and from the vicomte to their hostess. In the first moment of Pierreâ€™s outburst Anna PÃ¡vlovna, despite her social experience, was horror-struck. But when she saw that Pierreâ€™s sacrilegious words had not exasperated the vicomte, and had convinced herself that it was impossible to stop him, she rallied her forces and joined the vicomte in a vigorous attack on the orator.
â€œBut, my dear Monsieur Pierre,â€ said she, â€œhow do you explain the fact of a great man executing a ducâ€”or even an ordinary man whoâ€”is innocent and untried?â€
â€œI should like,â€ said the vicomte, â€œto ask how monsieur explains the 18th Brumaire; was not that an imposture? It was a swindle, and not at all like the conduct of a great man!â€
â€œAnd the prisoners he killed in Africa? That was horrible!â€ said the little princess, shrugging her shoulders.
â€œHeâ€™s a low fellow, say what you will,â€ remarked Prince Hippolyte.

Pierre, not knowing whom to answer, looked at them all and smiled. His smile was unlike the half-smile of other people. When he smiled, his grave, even rather gloomy, look was instantaneously replaced by anotherâ€”a childlike, kindly, even rather silly look, which seemed to ask forgiveness.
The vicomte who was meeting him for the first time saw clearly that this young Jacobin was not so terrible as his words suggested. All were silent.

â€œHow do you expect him to answer you all at once?â€ said Prince Andrew. â€œBesides, in the actions of a statesman one has to distinguish between his acts as a private person, as a general, and as an emperor. So it seems to me.â€
â€œYes, yes, of course!â€ Pierre chimed in, pleased at the arrival of this reinforcement.

â€œOne must admit,â€ continued Prince Andrew, â€œthat Napoleon as a man was great on the bridge of Arcola, and in the hospital at Jaffa where he gave his hand to the plague-stricken; but ... but there are other acts which it is difficult to justify.â€
Prince Andrew, who had evidently wished to tone down the awkwardness of Pierreâ€™s remarks, rose and made a sign to his wife that it was time to go.
Suddenly Prince Hippolyte started up making signs to everyone to attend, and asking them all to be seated began:

â€œI was told a charming Moscow story today and must treat you to it. Excuse me, Vicomteâ€”I must tell it in Russian or the point will be lost....â€ And Prince Hippolyte began to tell his story in such Russian as a Frenchman would speak after spending about a year in Russia. Everyone waited, so emphatically and eagerly did he demand their attention to his story.

â€œThere is in Moscow a lady, une dame, and she is very stingy. She must have two footmen behind her carriage, and very big ones. That was her taste. And she had a ladyâ€™s maid, also big. She said....â€
Here Prince Hippolyte paused, evidently collecting his ideas with difficulty.
â€œShe said.... Oh yes! She said, â€˜Girl,â€™ to the maid, â€˜put on a livery, get up behind the carriage, and come with me while I make some calls.â€™â€

Here Prince Hippolyte spluttered and burst out laughing long before his audience, which produced an effect unfavorable to the narrator. Several persons, among them the elderly lady and Anna PÃ¡vlovna, did however smile.

â€œShe went. Suddenly there was a great wind. The girl lost her hat and her long hair came down....â€ Here he could contain himself no longer and went on, between gasps of laughter: â€œAnd the whole world knew....â€

And so the anecdote ended. Though it was unintelligible why he had told it, or why it had to be told in Russian, still Anna PÃ¡vlovna and the others appreciated Prince Hippolyteâ€™s social tact in so agreeably ending Pierreâ€™s unpleasant and unamiable outburst. After the anecdote the conversation broke up into insignificant small talk about the last and next balls, about theatricals, and who would meet whom, and when and where.
Having thanked Anna PÃ¡vlovna for her charming soiree, the guests began to take their leave.

payload.msg: Pierre was ungainly. Stout, about the average height, broad, with huge red hands; he did not know, as the saying is, how to enter a drawing room and still less how to leave one; that is, how to say something particularly agreeable before going away. Besides this he was absent-minded. When he rose to go, he took up instead of his own, the generalâ€™s three-cornered hat, and held it, pulling at the plume, till the general asked him to restore it. All his absent-mindedness and inability to enter a room and converse in it was, however, redeemed by his kindly, simple, and modest expression. Anna PÃ¡vlovna turned toward him and, with a Christian mildness that expressed forgiveness of his indiscretion, nodded and said: â€œI hope to see you again, but I also hope you will change your opinions, my dear Monsieur Pierre.â€

When she said this, he did not reply and only bowed, but again everybody saw his smile, which said nothing, unless perhaps, â€œOpinions are opinions, but you see what a capital, good-natured fellow I am.â€ And everyone, including Anna PÃ¡vlovna, felt this.

Prince Andrew had gone out into the hall, and, turning his shoulders to the footman who was helping him on with his cloak, listened indifferently to his wifeâ€™s chatter with Prince Hippolyte who had also come into the hall. Prince Hippolyte stood close to the pretty, pregnant princess, and stared fixedly at her through his eyeglass.
â€œGo in, Annette, or you will catch cold,â€ said the little princess, taking leave of Anna PÃ¡vlovna. â€œIt is settled,â€ she added in a low voice.

Two footmen, the princessâ€™ and his own, stood holding a shawl and a cloak, waiting for the conversation to finish. â€œI am very glad I did not go to the ambassadorâ€™s,â€ said Prince Hippolyte â€œâ€”so dullâ€”. It has been a delightful evening, has it not? Delightful!â€
â€œThey say the ball will be very good,â€ replied the princess, drawing up her downy little lip. â€œAll the pretty women in society will be there.â€

Either from awkwardness or intentionally (no one could have said which) after the shawl had been adjusted he kept his arm around her for a long time, as though embracing her.
Still smiling, she gracefully moved away, turning and glancing at her husband. Prince Andrewâ€™s eyes were closed, so weary and sleepy did he seem.
â€œAre you ready?â€ he asked his wife, looking past her.

Prince Hippolyte hurriedly put on his cloak, which in the latest fashion reached to his very heels, and, stumbling in it, ran out into the porch following the princess, whom a footman was helping into the carriage.
â€œPrincesse, au revoir,â€ cried he, stumbling with his tongue as well as with his feet.
The princess, picking up her dress, was taking her seat in the dark carriage, her husband was adjusting his saber; Prince Hippolyte, under pretense of helping, was in everyoneâ€™s way.
â€œAllow me, sir,â€ said Prince Andrew in Russian in a cold, disagreeable tone to Prince Hippolyte who was blocking his path.
â€œI am expecting you, Pierre,â€ said the same voice, but gently and affectionately.
The postilion started, the carriage wheels rattled. Prince Hippolyte laughed spasmodically as he stood in the porch waiting for the vicomte whom he had promised to take home.

â€œWell, mon cher,â€ said the vicomte, having seated himself beside Hippolyte in the carriage, â€œyour little princess is very nice, very nice indeed, quite French,â€ and he kissed the tips of his fingers. Hippolyte burst out laughing.
â€œDo you know, you are a terrible chap for all your innocent airs,â€ continued the vicomte. â€œI pity the poor husband, that little officer who gives himself the airs of a monarch.â€
Hippolyte spluttered again, and amid his laughter said, â€œAnd you were saying that the Russian ladies are not equal to the French? One has to know how to deal with them.â€

Pierre reaching the house first went into Prince Andrewâ€™s study like one quite at home, and from habit immediately lay down on the sofa, took from the shelf the first book that came to his hand (it was Caesarâ€™s Commentaries), and resting on his elbow, began reading it in the middle.
â€œWhat have you done to Mlle SchÃ©rer? She will be quite ill now,â€ said Prince Andrew, as he entered the study, rubbing his small white hands.

â€œThat abbÃ© is very interesting but he does not see the thing in the right light.... In my opinion perpetual peace is possible butâ€”I do not know how to express it ... not by a balance of political power....â€

Pierre at the age of ten had been sent abroad with an abbÃ© as tutor, and had remained away till he was twenty. When he returned to Moscow his father dismissed the abbÃ© and said to the young man, â€œNow go to Petersburg, look round, and choose your profession. I will agree to anything. Here is a letter to Prince VasÃ­li, and here is money. Write to me all about it, and I will help you in everything.â€ Pierre had already been choosing a career for three months, and had not decided on anything. It was about this choice that Prince Andrew was speaking. Pierre rubbed his forehead.
â€œBut he must be a Freemason,â€ said he, referring to the abbÃ© whom he had met that evening.
â€œThat is all nonsense.â€ Prince Andrew again interrupted him, â€œlet us talk business. Have you been to the Horse Guards?â€

â€œNo, I have not; but this is what I have been thinking and wanted to tell you. There is a war now against Napoleon. If it were a war for freedom I could understand it and should be the first to enter the army; but to help England and Austria against the greatest man in the world is not right.â€

Prince Andrew only shrugged his shoulders at Pierreâ€™s childish words. He put on the air of one who finds it impossible to reply to such nonsense, but it would in fact have been difficult to give any other answer than the one Prince Andrew gave to this naÃ¯ve question.
â€œIf no one fought except on his own conviction, there would be no wars,â€ he said.
â€œAnd that would be splendid,â€ said Pierre.
â€œVery likely it would be splendid, but it will never come about....â€
â€œWell, why are you going to the war?â€ asked Pierre.
â€œWhat for? I donâ€™t know. I must. Besides that I am going....â€ He paused. â€œI am going because the life I am leading here does not suit me!â€

The rustle of a womanâ€™s dress was heard in the next room. Prince Andrew shook himself as if waking up, and his face assumed the look it had had in Anna PÃ¡vlovnaâ€™s drawing room. Pierre removed his feet from the sofa. The princess came in. She had changed her gown for a house dress as fresh and elegant as the other. Prince Andrew rose and politely placed a chair for her.

â€œHow is it,â€ she began, as usual in French, settling down briskly and fussily in the easy chair, â€œhow is it Annette never got married? How stupid you men all are not to have married her! Excuse me for saying so, but you have no sense about women. What an argumentative fellow you are, Monsieur Pierre!â€

â€œAnd I am still arguing with your husband. I canâ€™t understand why he wants to go to the war,â€ replied Pierre, addressing the princess with none of the embarrassment so commonly shown by young men in their intercourse with young women.
The princess started. Evidently Pierreâ€™s words touched her to the quick.

â€œAh, that is just what I tell him!â€ said she. â€œI donâ€™t understand it; I donâ€™t in the least understand why men canâ€™t live without wars. How is it that we women donâ€™t want anything of the kind, donâ€™t need it? Now you shall judge between us. I always tell him: Here he is Uncleâ€™s aide-de-camp, a most brilliant position. He is so well known, so much appreciated by everyone. The other day at the AprÃ¡ksinsâ€™ I heard a lady asking, â€˜Is that the famous Prince Andrew?â€™ I did indeed.â€ She laughed. â€œHe is so well received everywhere. He might easily become aide-de-camp to the Emperor. You know the Emperor spoke to him most graciously. Annette and I were speaking of how to arrange it. What do you think?â€
Pierre looked at his friend and, noticing that he did not like the conversation, gave no reply.
â€œWhen are you starting?â€ he asked.

payload.msg: â€œOh, donâ€™t speak of his going, donâ€™t! I wonâ€™t hear it spoken of,â€ said the princess in the same petulantly playful tone in which she had spoken to Hippolyte in the drawing room and which was so plainly ill-suited to the family circle of which Pierre was almost a member. â€œToday when I remembered that all these delightful associations must be broken off ... and then you know, AndrÃ©...â€ (she looked significantly at her husband) â€œIâ€™m afraid, Iâ€™m afraid!â€ she whispered, and a shudder ran down her back.

â€œWhat is it you are afraid of, Lise? I donâ€™t understand,â€ said he.
â€œThere, what egotists men all are: all, all egotists! Just for a whim of his own, goodness only knows why, he leaves me and locks me up alone in the country.â€

â€œAlone all the same, without my friends.... And he expects me not to be afraid.â€

â€œYour doctor tells you to go to bed earlier,â€ said Prince Andrew. â€œYou had better go.â€
The princess said nothing, but suddenly her short downy lip quivered. Prince Andrew rose, shrugged his shoulders, and walked about the room.
Pierre looked over his spectacles with naÃ¯ve surprise, now at him and now at her, moved as if about to rise too, but changed his mind.

â€œWhy should I mind Monsieur Pierre being here?â€ exclaimed the little princess suddenly, her pretty face all at once distorted by a tearful grimace. â€œI have long wanted to ask you, Andrew, why you have changed so to me? What have I done to you? You are going to the war and have no pity for me. Why is it?â€
â€œLise!â€ was all Prince Andrew said. But that one word expressed an entreaty, a threat, and above all conviction that she would herself regret her words. But she went on hurriedly:
â€œYou treat me like an invalid or a child. I see it all! Did you behave like that six months ago?â€
â€œLise, I beg you to desist,â€ said Prince Andrew still more emphatically.
Pierre, who had been growing more and more agitated as he listened to all this, rose and approached the princess. He seemed unable to bear the sight of tears and was ready to cry himself.

â€œCalm yourself, Princess! It seems so to you because.... I assure you I myself have experienced ... and so ... because ... No, excuse me! An outsider is out of place here.... No, donâ€™t distress yourself.... Good-by!â€

â€œNo, wait, Pierre! The princess is too kind to wish to deprive me of the pleasure of spending the evening with you.â€
â€œNo, he thinks only of himself,â€ muttered the princess without restraining her angry tears.
â€œLise!â€ said Prince Andrew dryly, raising his voice to the pitch which indicates that patience is exhausted.

Suddenly the angry, squirrel-like expression of the princessâ€™ pretty face changed into a winning and piteous look of fear. Her beautiful eyes glanced askance at her husbandâ€™s face, and her own assumed the timid, deprecating expression of a dog when it rapidly but feebly wags its drooping tail.
â€œMon Dieu, mon Dieu!â€ she muttered, and lifting her dress with one hand she went up to her husband and kissed him on the forehead.

The friends were silent. Neither cared to begin talking. Pierre continually glanced at Prince Andrew; Prince Andrew rubbed his forehead with his small hand.
â€œLet us go and have supper,â€ he said with a sigh, going to the door.

They entered the elegant, newly decorated, and luxurious dining room. Everything from the table napkins to the silver, china, and glass bore that imprint of newness found in the households of the newly married. Halfway through supper Prince Andrew leaned his elbows on the table and, with a look of nervous agitation such as Pierre had never before seen on his face, began to talkâ€”as one who has long had something on his mind and suddenly determines to speak out.

â€œNever, never marry, my dear fellow! Thatâ€™s my advice: never marry till you can say to yourself that you have done all you are capable of, and until you have ceased to love the woman of your choice and have seen her plainly as she is, or else you will make a cruel and irrevocable mistake. Marry when you are old and good for nothingâ€”or all that is good and noble in you will be lost. It will all be wasted on trifles. Yes! Yes! Yes! Donâ€™t look at me with such surprise. If you marry expecting anything from yourself in the future, you will feel at every step that for you all is ended, all is closed except the drawing room, where you will be ranged side by side with a court lackey and an idiot!... But whatâ€™s the good?...â€ and he waved his arm.
Pierre took off his spectacles, which made his face seem different and the good-natured expression still more apparent, and gazed at his friend in amazement.

â€œMy wife,â€ continued Prince Andrew, â€œis an excellent woman, one of those rare women with whom a manâ€™s honor is safe; but, O God, what would I not give now to be unmarried! You are the first and only one to whom I mention this, because I like you.â€

As he said this Prince Andrew was less than ever like that BolkÃ³nski who had lolled in Anna PÃ¡vlovnaâ€™s easy chairs and with half-closed eyes had uttered French phrases between his teeth. Every muscle of his thin face was now quivering with nervous excitement; his eyes, in which the fire of life had seemed extinguished, now flashed with brilliant light. It was evident that the more lifeless he seemed at ordinary times, the more impassioned he became in these moments of almost morbid irritation.

â€œYou donâ€™t understand why I say this,â€ he continued, â€œbut it is the whole story of life. You talk of Bonaparte and his career,â€ said he (though Pierre had not mentioned Bonaparte), â€œbut Bonaparte when he worked went step by step toward his goal. He was free, he had nothing but his aim to consider, and he reached it. But tie yourself up with a woman and, like a chained convict, you lose all freedom! And all you have of hope and strength merely weighs you down and torments you with regret. Drawing rooms, gossip, balls, vanity, and trivialityâ€”these are the enchanted circle I cannot escape from. I am now going to the war, the greatest war there ever was, and I know nothing and am fit for nothing. I am very amiable and have a caustic wit,â€ continued Prince Andrew, â€œand at Anna PÃ¡vlovnaâ€™s they listen to me. And that stupid set without whom my wife cannot exist, and those women.... If you only knew what those society women are, and women in general! My father is right. Selfish, vain, stupid, trivial in everythingâ€”thatâ€™s what women are when you see them in their true colors! When you meet them in society it seems as if there were something in them, but thereâ€™s nothing, nothing, nothing! No, donâ€™t marry, my dear fellow; donâ€™t marry!â€ concluded Prince Andrew.
â€œIt seems funny to me,â€ said Pierre, â€œthat you, you should consider yourself incapable and your life a spoiled life. You have everything before you, everything. And you....â€
He did not finish his sentence, but his tone showed how highly he thought of his friend and how much he expected of him in the future.

â€œHow can he talk like that?â€ thought Pierre. He considered his friend a model of perfection because Prince Andrew possessed in the highest degree just the very qualities Pierre lacked, and which might be best described as strength of will. Pierre was always astonished at Prince Andrewâ€™s calm manner of treating everybody, his extraordinary memory, his extensive reading (he had read everything, knew everything, and had an opinion about everything), but above all at his capacity for work and study. And if Pierre was often struck by Andrewâ€™s lack of capacity for philosophical meditation (to which he himself was particularly addicted), he regarded even this not as a defect but as a sign of strength.
Even in the best, most friendly and simplest relations of life, praise and commendation are essential, just as grease is necessary to wheels that they may run smoothly.
â€œMy part is played out,â€ said Prince Andrew. â€œWhatâ€™s the use of talking about me? Let us talk about you,â€ he added after a silence, smiling at his reassuring thoughts.
That smile was immediately reflected on Pierreâ€™s face.

â€œBut what is there to say about me?â€ said Pierre, his face relaxing into a careless, merry smile. â€œWhat am I? An illegitimate son!â€ He suddenly blushed crimson, and it was plain that he had made a great effort to say this. â€œWithout a name and without means... And it really...â€ But he did not say what â€œit reallyâ€ was. â€œFor the present I am free and am all right. Only I havenâ€™t the least idea what I am to do; I wanted to consult you seriously.â€
Prince Andrew looked kindly at him, yet his glanceâ€”friendly and affectionate as it wasâ€”expressed a sense of his own superiority.

â€œI am fond of you, especially as you are the one live man among our whole set. Yes, youâ€™re all right! Choose what you will; itâ€™s all the same. Youâ€™ll be all right anywhere. But look here: give up visiting those KurÃ¡gins and leading that sort of life. It suits you so badlyâ€”all this debauchery, dissipation, and the rest of it!â€
â€œWhat would you have, my dear fellow?â€ answered Pierre, shrugging his shoulders. â€œWomen, my dear fellow; women!â€
â€œI donâ€™t understand it,â€ replied Prince Andrew. â€œWomen who are comme il faut, thatâ€™s a different matter; but the KurÃ¡ginsâ€™ set of women, â€˜women and wineâ€™ I donâ€™t understand!â€
Pierre was staying at Prince VasÃ­li KurÃ¡ginâ€™s and sharing the dissipated life of his son Anatole, the son whom they were planning to reform by marrying him to Prince Andrewâ€™s sister.

â€œDo you know?â€ said Pierre, as if suddenly struck by a happy thought, â€œseriously, I have long been thinking of it.... Leading such a life I canâ€™t decide or think properly about anything. Oneâ€™s head aches, and one spends all oneâ€™s money. He asked me for tonight, but I wonâ€™t go.â€

payload.msg: It was past one oâ€™clock when Pierre left his friend. It was a cloudless, northern, summer night. Pierre took an open cab intending to drive straight home. But the nearer he drew to the house the more he felt the impossibility of going to sleep on such a night. It was light enough to see a long way in the deserted street and it seemed more like morning or evening than night. On the way Pierre remembered that Anatole KurÃ¡gin was expecting the usual set for cards that evening, after which there was generally a drinking bout, finishing with visits of a kind Pierre was very fond of.
â€œI should like to go to KurÃ¡ginâ€™s,â€ thought he.

But he immediately recalled his promise to Prince Andrew not to go there. Then, as happens to people of weak character, he desired so passionately once more to enjoy that dissipation he was so accustomed to that he decided to go. The thought immediately occurred to him that his promise to Prince Andrew was of no account, because before he gave it he had already promised Prince Anatole to come to his gathering; â€œbesides,â€ thought he, â€œall such â€˜words of honorâ€™ are conventional things with no definite meaning, especially if one considers that by tomorrow one may be dead, or something so extraordinary may happen to one that honor and dishonor will be all the same!â€ Pierre often indulged in reflections of this sort, nullifying all his decisions and intentions. He went to KurÃ¡ginâ€™s.

Reaching the large house near the Horse Guardsâ€™ barracks, in which Anatole lived, Pierre entered the lighted porch, ascended the stairs, and went in at the open door. There was no one in the anteroom; empty bottles, cloaks, and overshoes were lying about; there was a smell of alcohol, and sounds of voices and shouting in the distance.

Cards and supper were over, but the visitors had not yet dispersed. Pierre threw off his cloak and entered the first room, in which were the remains of supper. A footman, thinking no one saw him, was drinking on the sly what was left in the glasses. From the third room came sounds of laughter, the shouting of familiar voices, the growling of a bear, and general commotion. Some eight or nine young men were crowding anxiously round an open window. Three others were romping with a young bear, one pulling him by the chain and trying to set him at the others.
â€œI bet a hundred on Stevens!â€ shouted one.

Another voice, from a man of medium height with clear blue eyes, particularly striking among all these drunken voices by its sober ring, cried from the window: â€œCome here; part the bets!â€ This was DÃ³lokhov, an officer of the SemÃ«nov regiment, a notorious gambler and duelist, who was living with Anatole. Pierre smiled, looking about him merrily.
â€œI donâ€™t understand. Whatâ€™s it all about?â€

DÃ³lokhov was of medium height, with curly hair and light-blue eyes. He was about twenty-five. The bottle of rum was brought. The window frame which prevented anyone from sitting on the outer sill was being forced out by two footmen, who were evidently flurried and intimidated by the directions and shouts of the gentlemen around.

â€œYou have a try, Hercules,â€ said he, turning to Pierre.

The Englishman nodded, but gave no indication whether he intended to accept this challenge or not. Suddenly DÃ³lokhov made a backward movement with his spine, and his arm trembled nervously; this was sufficient to cause his whole body to slip as he sat on the sloping ledge. The Englishman took out his purse and began counting out the money. DÃ³lokhov stood frowning and did not speak. Pierre jumped upon the window sill.
â€œGentlemen, who wishes to bet with me? Iâ€™ll do the same thing!â€ he suddenly cried. â€œEven without a bet, there! Tell them to bring me a bottle. Iâ€™ll do it.... Bring a bottle!â€
â€œLet him do it, let him do it,â€ said DÃ³lokhov, smiling.

â€œI am very, very grateful to you, mon cher,â€ or â€œma chÃ¨reâ€â€”he called everyone without exception and without the slightest variation in his tone, â€œmy dear,â€ whether they were above or below him in rankâ€”â€œI thank you for myself and for our two dear ones whose name day we are keeping. payload.msg: Meanwhile the younger generation: BorÃ­s, the officer, Anna MikhÃ¡ylovnaâ€™s son; Nicholas, the undergraduate, the countâ€™s eldest son; SÃ³nya, the countâ€™s fifteen-year-old niece, and little PÃ©tya, his youngest boy, had all settled down in the drawing room and were obviously trying to restrain within the bounds of decorum the excitement and mirth that shone in all their faces. Evidently in the back rooms, from which they had dashed out so impetuously, the conversation had been more amusing than the drawing room talk of society scandals, the weather, and Countess AprÃ¡ksina. Now and then they glanced at one another, hardly able to suppress their laughter.

The two young men, the student and the officer, friends from childhood, were of the same age and both handsome fellows, though not alike. BorÃ­s was tall and fair, and his calm and handsome face had regular, delicate features. Nicholas was short with curly hair and an open expression. Dark hairs were already showing on his upper lip, and his whole face expressed impetuosity and enthusiasm. Nicholas blushed when he entered the drawing room. He evidently tried to find something to say, but failed. BorÃ­s on the contrary at once found his footing, and related quietly and humorously how he had known that doll Mimi when she was still quite a young lady, before her nose was broken; how she had aged during the five years he had known her, and how her head had cracked right across the skull. Having said this he glanced at NatÃ¡sha. She turned away from him and glanced at her younger brother, who was screwing up his eyes and shaking with suppressed laughter, and unable to control herself any longer, she jumped up and rushed from the room as fast as her nimble little feet would carry her. BorÃ­s did not laugh.
â€œYou were meaning to go out, werenâ€™t you, Mamma? Do you want the carriage?â€ he asked his mother with a smile.
â€œYes, yes, go and tell them to get it ready,â€ she answered, returning his smile.
BorÃ­s quietly left the room and went in search of NatÃ¡sha. The plump boy ran after them angrily, as if vexed that their program had been disturbed.

The only young people remaining in the drawing room, not counting the young lady visitor and the countessâ€™ eldest daughter (who was four years older than her sister and behaved already like a grown-up person), were Nicholas and SÃ³nya, the niece. SÃ³nya was a slender little brunette with a tender look in her eyes which were veiled by long lashes, thick black plaits coiling twice round her head, and a tawny tint in her complexion and especially in the color of her slender but graceful and muscular arms and neck. â€œAh yes, my dear,â€ said the count, addressing the visitor and pointing to Nicholas, â€œhis friend BorÃ­s has become an officer, and so for friendshipâ€™s sake he is leaving the university and me, his old father, and entering the military service, my dear. â€œSchubert, the colonel of the PÃ¡vlograd Hussars, is dining with us today. He has been here on leave and is taking Nicholas back with him. It canâ€™t be helped!â€ said the count, shrugging his shoulders and speaking playfully of a matter that evidently distressed him.

But I know I am no use anywhere except in the army; I am not a diplomat or a government clerk.â€”I donâ€™t know how to hide what I feel.â€ As he spoke he kept glancing with the flirtatiousness of a handsome youth at SÃ³nya and the young lady visitor.
The little kitten, feasting her eyes on him, seemed ready at any moment to start her gambols again and display her kittenish nature.

â€œAll right, all right!â€ said the old count. â€œHe always flares up! This Buonaparte has turned all their heads; they all think of how he rose from an ensign and became Emperor. Well, well, God grant it,â€ he added, not noticing his visitorâ€™s sarcastic smile.
The elders began talking about Bonaparte. Julie KarÃ¡gina turned to young RostÃ³v.

The young man, flattered, sat down nearer to her with a coquettish smile, and engaged the smiling Julie in a confidential conversation without at all noticing that his involuntary smile had stabbed the heart of SÃ³nya, who blushed and smiled unnaturally. â€œHow plainly all these young people wear their hearts on their sleeves!â€ said Anna MikhÃ¡ylovna, pointing to Nicholas as he went out. â€œCousinageâ€”dangereux voisinage,â€ * she added.

â€œYes, youâ€™re quite right,â€ continued the countess. â€œTill now I have always, thank God, been my childrenâ€™s friend and had their full confidence,â€ said she, repeating the mistake of so many parents who imagine that their children have no secrets from them. â€œI know I shall always be my daughtersâ€™ first confidante, and that if Nicholas, with his impulsive nature, does get into mischief (a boy canâ€™t help it), he will all the same never be like those Petersburg young men.â€

â€œYes, they are splendid, splendid youngsters,â€ chimed in the count, who always solved questions that seemed to him perplexing by deciding that everything was splendid. â€œJust fancy: wants to be an hussar. Whatâ€™s one to do, my dear?â€

payload.msg: â€œWhat is the something?â€ asked he.

args.text: In feverish tones:.

payload.msg: â€œAh, my dear,â€ said the countess, â€œmy life is not all roses either. Donâ€™t I know that at the rate we are living our means wonâ€™t last long? â€œAh, my love,â€ answered Anna MikhÃ¡ylovna, â€œGod grant you never know what it is to be left a widow without means and with a son you love to distraction! â€œHas Prince VasÃ­li aged much?â€ asked the countess. â€œI have not seen him since we acted together at the RumyÃ¡ntsovsâ€™ theatricals. I expect he has forgotten me. He paid me attentions in those days,â€ said the countess, with a smile.

args.text: To rebellion.

payload.msg: â€œWould not such a meeting be too trying for him, dear Anna MikhÃ¡ylovna?â€ said he. â€œLet us wait until evening. The doctors are expecting a crisis.â€

â€œStill the same; but what can you expect, this noise...â€ said the princess, looking at Anna MikhÃ¡ylovna as at a stranger.

He sent for Pierre and said to him: â€œMy dear fellow, if you are going to behave here as you did in Petersburg, you will end very badly; that is all I have to say to you. The count is very, very ill, and you must not see him at all.â€
Since then Pierre had not been disturbed and had spent the whole time in his rooms upstairs.

When BorÃ­s appeared at his door Pierre was pacing up and down his room, stopping occasionally at a corner to make menacing gestures at the wall, as if running a sword through an invisible foe, and glaring savagely over his spectacles, and then again resuming his walk, muttering indistinct words, shrugging his shoulders and gesticulating.

â€œEngland is done for,â€ said he, scowling and pointing his finger at someone unseen. â€œMr. Pitt, as a traitor to the nation and to the rights of man, is sentenced to...â€ But before Pierreâ€”who at that moment imagined himself to be Napoleon in person and to have just effected the dangerous crossing of the Straits of Dover and captured Londonâ€”could pronounce Pittâ€™s sentence, he saw a well-built and handsome young officer entering his room. Pierre paused. He had left Moscow when BorÃ­s was a boy of fourteen, and had quite forgotten him, but in his usual impulsive and hearty way he took BorÃ­s by the hand with a friendly smile.
â€œDo you remember me?â€ asked BorÃ­s quietly with a pleasant smile. â€œI have come with my mother to see the count, but it seems he is not well.â€
â€œYes, it seems he is ill. People are always disturbing him,â€ answered Pierre, trying to remember who this young man was.
BorÃ­s felt that Pierre did not recognize him but did not consider it necessary to introduce himself, and without experiencing the least embarrassment looked Pierre straight in the face.
â€œCount RostÃ³v asks you to come to dinner today,â€ said he, after a considerable pause which made Pierre feel uncomfortable.

â€œAh, Count RostÃ³v!â€ exclaimed Pierre joyfully. â€œThen you are his son, IlyÃ¡? Only fancy, I didnâ€™t know you at first. Do you remember how we went to the Sparrow Hills with Madame Jacquot?... Itâ€™s such an age...â€

â€œYou are mistaken,â€ said BorÃ­s deliberately, with a bold and slightly sarcastic smile. â€œI am BorÃ­s, son of Princess Anna MikhÃ¡ylovna DrubetskÃ¡ya. RostÃ³v, the father, is IlyÃ¡, and his son is Nicholas. I never knew any Madame Jacquot.â€
Pierre shook his head and arms as if attacked by mosquitoes or bees.

â€œOh dear, what am I thinking about? Iâ€™ve mixed everything up. One has so many relatives in Moscow! So you are BorÃ­s? Of course. Well, now we know where we are. And what do you think of the Boulogne expedition? The English will come off badly, you know, if Napoleon gets across the Channel. I think the expedition is quite feasible. If only Villeneuve doesnâ€™t make a mess of things!â€
BorÃ­s knew nothing about the Boulogne expedition; he did not read the papers and it was the first time he had heard Villeneuveâ€™s name.

â€œWe here in Moscow are more occupied with dinner parties and scandal than with politics,â€ said he in his quiet ironical tone. â€œI know nothing about it and have not thought about it. Moscow is chiefly busy with gossip,â€ he continued. â€œJust now they are talking about you and your father.â€

Pierre smiled in his good-natured way as if afraid for his companionâ€™s sake that the latter might say something he would afterwards regret. But BorÃ­s spoke distinctly, clearly, and dryly, looking straight into Pierreâ€™s eyes.

â€œMoscow has nothing else to do but gossip,â€ BorÃ­s went on. â€œEverybody is wondering to whom the count will leave his fortune, though he may perhaps outlive us all, as I sincerely hope he will...â€
â€œYes, it is all very horrid,â€ interrupted Pierre, â€œvery horrid.â€
Pierre was still afraid that this officer might inadvertently say something disconcerting to himself.
â€œAnd it must seem to you,â€ said BorÃ­s flushing slightly, but not changing his tone or attitude, â€œit must seem to you that everyone is trying to get something out of the rich man?â€

â€œBut I just wish to say, to avoid misunderstandings, that you are quite mistaken if you reckon me or my mother among such people. We are very poor, but for my own part at any rate, for the very reason that your father is rich, I donâ€™t regard myself as a relation of his, and neither I nor my mother would ever ask or take anything from him.â€

For a long time Pierre could not understand, but when he did, he jumped up from the sofa, seized BorÃ­s under the elbow in his quick, clumsy way, and, blushing far more than BorÃ­s, began to speak with a feeling of mingled shame and vexation.
â€œWell, this is strange! Do you suppose I... who could think?... I know very well...â€

â€œI am glad I have spoken out fully. Perhaps you did not like it? You must excuse me,â€ said he, putting Pierre at ease instead of being put at ease by him, â€œbut I hope I have not offended you. I always make it a rule to speak out... Well, what answer am I to take? Will you come to dinner at the RostÃ³vsâ€™?â€
And BorÃ­s, having apparently relieved himself of an onerous duty and extricated himself from an awkward situation and placed another in it, became quite pleasant again.

â€œNo, but I say,â€ said Pierre, calming down, â€œyou are a wonderful fellow! What you have just said is good, very good. Of course you donâ€™t know me. We have not met for such a long time... not since we were children. You might think that I... I understand, quite understand. I could not have done it myself, I should not have had the courage, but itâ€™s splendid. I am very glad to have made your acquaintance. Itâ€™s queer,â€ he added after a pause, â€œthat you should have suspected me!â€ He began to laugh. â€œWell, what of it! I hope weâ€™ll get better acquainted,â€ and he pressed BorÃ­sâ€™ hand. â€œDo you know, I have not once been in to see the count. He has not sent for me.... I am sorry for him as a man, but what can one do?â€
â€œAnd so you think Napoleon will manage to get an army across?â€ asked BorÃ­s with a smile.
Pierre saw that BorÃ­s wished to change the subject, and being of the same mind he began explaining the advantages and disadvantages of the Boulogne expedition.

A footman came in to summon BorÃ­sâ€”the princess was going. Pierre, in order to make BorÃ­sâ€™ better acquaintance, promised to come to dinner, and warmly pressing his hand looked affectionately over his spectacles into BorÃ­sâ€™ eyes. After he had gone Pierre continued pacing up and down the room for a long time, no longer piercing an imaginary foe with his imaginary sword, but smiling at the remembrance of that pleasant, intelligent, and resolute young man.
As often happens in early youth, especially to one who leads a lonely life, he felt an unaccountable tenderness for this young man and made up his mind that they would be friends.

payload.msg: Prince VasÃ­li saw the princess off. She held a handkerchief to her eyes and her face was tearful.

â€œIt is dreadful, dreadful!â€ she was saying, â€œbut cost me what it may I shall do my duty. I will come and spend the night. He must not be left like this. Every moment is precious. I canâ€™t think why his nieces put it off. Perhaps God will help me to find a way to prepare him!... Adieu, Prince! May God support you...â€
â€œAdieu, ma bonne,â€ answered Prince VasÃ­li turning away from her.
â€œOh, he is in a dreadful state,â€ said the mother to her son when they were in the carriage. â€œHe hardly recognizes anybody.â€
â€œI donâ€™t understand, Mammaâ€”what is his attitude to Pierre?â€ asked the son.
â€œThe will will show that, my dear; our fate also depends on it.â€
â€œBut why do you expect that he will leave us anything?â€
â€œAh, my dear! He is so rich, and we are so poor!â€
â€œWell, that is hardly a sufficient reason, Mamma...â€
â€œOh, Heaven! How ill he is!â€ exclaimed the mother.

After Anna MikhÃ¡ylovna had driven off with her son to visit Count Cyril VladÃ­mirovich BezÃºkhov, Countess RostÃ³va sat for a long time all alone applying her handkerchief to her eyes. At last she rang.
â€œWhat is the matter with you, my dear?â€ she said crossly to the maid who kept her waiting some minutes. â€œDonâ€™t you wish to serve me? Then Iâ€™ll find you another place.â€

The countess was upset by her friendâ€™s sorrow and humiliating poverty, and was therefore out of sorts, a state of mind which with her always found expression in calling her maid â€œmy dearâ€ and speaking to her with exaggerated politeness.
â€œI am very sorry, maâ€™am,â€ answered the maid.
â€œAsk the count to come to me.â€
The count came waddling in to see his wife with a rather guilty look as usual.
â€œWell, little countess? What a sautÃ© of game au madÃ¨re we are to have, my dear! I tasted it. The thousand rubles I paid for TarÃ¡s were not ill-spent. He is worth it!â€
He sat down by his wife, his elbows on his knees and his hands ruffling his gray hair.
â€œWhat are your commands, little countess?â€
â€œYou see, my dear... Whatâ€™s that mess?â€ she said, pointing to his waistcoat. â€œItâ€™s the sautÃ©, most likely,â€ she added with a smile. â€œWell, you see, Count, I want some money.â€
â€œOh, little countess!â€ ... and the count began bustling to get out his pocketbook.
â€œI want a great deal, Count! I want five hundred rubles,â€ and taking out her cambric handkerchief she began wiping her husbandâ€™s waistcoat.
â€œYes, immediately, immediately! Hey, whoâ€™s there?â€ he called out in a tone only used by persons who are certain that those they call will rush to obey the summons. â€œSend DmÃ­tri to me!â€
DmÃ­tri, a man of good family who had been brought up in the countâ€™s house and now managed all his affairs, stepped softly into the room.

â€œThis is what I want, my dear fellow,â€ said the count to the deferential young man who had entered. â€œBring me...â€ he reflected a moment, â€œyes, bring me seven hundred rubles, yes! But mind, donâ€™t bring me such tattered and dirty notes as last time, but nice clean ones for the countess.â€
â€œYes, DmÃ­tri, clean ones, please,â€ said the countess, sighing deeply.

â€œWhen would you like them, your excellency?â€ asked DmÃ­tri. â€œAllow me to inform you... But, donâ€™t be uneasy,â€ he added, noticing that the count was beginning to breathe heavily and quickly which was always a sign of approaching anger. â€œI was forgetting... Do you wish it brought at once?â€
â€œYes, yes; just so! Bring it. Give it to the countess.â€
â€œWhat a treasure that DmÃ­tri is,â€ added the count with a smile when the young man had departed. â€œThere is never any â€˜impossibleâ€™ with him. Thatâ€™s a thing I hate! Everything is possible.â€
â€œAh, money, Count, money! How much sorrow it causes in the world,â€ said the countess. â€œBut I am in great need of this sum.â€
â€œYou, my little countess, are a notorious spendthrift,â€ said the count, and having kissed his wifeâ€™s hand he went back to his study.

When Anna MikhÃ¡ylovna returned from Count BezÃºkhovâ€™s the money, all in clean notes, was lying ready under a handkerchief on the countessâ€™ little table, and Anna MikhÃ¡ylovna noticed that something was agitating her.
â€œWell, my dear?â€ asked the countess.
â€œOh, what a terrible state he is in! One would not know him, he is so ill! I was only there a few moments and hardly said a word...â€

â€œAnnette, for heavenâ€™s sake donâ€™t refuse me,â€ the countess began, with a blush that looked very strange on her thin, dignified, elderly face, and she took the money from under the handkerchief.
Anna MikhÃ¡ylovna instantly guessed her intention and stooped to be ready to embrace the countess at the appropriate moment.
â€œThis is for BorÃ­s from me, for his outfit.â€

Anna MikhÃ¡ylovna was already embracing her and weeping. The countess wept too. They wept because they were friends, and because they were kindhearted, and because theyâ€”friends from childhoodâ€”had to think about such a base thing as money, and because their youth was over.... But those tears were pleasant to them both.

Countess RostÃ³va, with her daughters and a large number of guests, was already seated in the drawing room. The count took the gentlemen into his study and showed them his choice collection of Turkish pipes. From time to time he went out to ask: â€œHasnâ€™t she come yet?â€ They were expecting MÃ¡rya DmÃ­trievna AkhrosÃ­mova, known in society as le terrible dragon, a lady distinguished not for wealth or rank, but for common sense and frank plainness of speech. MÃ¡rya DmÃ­trievna was known to the Imperial family as well as to all Moscow and Petersburg, and both cities wondered at her, laughed privately at her rudenesses, and told good stories about her, while none the less all without exception respected and feared her.

In the countâ€™s room, which was full of tobacco smoke, they talked of the war that had been announced in a manifesto, and about the recruiting. None of them had yet seen the manifesto, but they all knew it had appeared. The count sat on the sofa between two guests who were smoking and talking. He neither smoked nor talked, but bending his head first to one side and then to the other watched the smokers with evident pleasure and listened to the conversation of his two neighbors, whom he egged on against each other.

One of them was a sallow, clean-shaven civilian with a thin and wrinkled face, already growing old, though he was dressed like a most fashionable young man. He sat with his legs up on the sofa as if quite at home and, having stuck an amber mouthpiece far into his mouth, was inhaling the smoke spasmodically and screwing up his eyes. This was an old bachelor, ShinshÃ­n, a cousin of the countessâ€™, a man with â€œa sharp tongueâ€ as they said in Moscow society. He seemed to be condescending to his companion. The latter, a fresh, rosy officer of the Guards, irreproachably washed, brushed, and buttoned, held his pipe in the middle of his mouth and with red lips gently inhaled the smoke, letting it escape from his handsome mouth in rings. This was Lieutenant Berg, an officer in the SemÃ«nov regiment with whom BorÃ­s was to travel to join the army, and about whom NatÃ¡sha had teased her elder sister VÃ©ra, speaking of Berg as her â€œintended.â€ The count sat between them and listened attentively. His favorite occupation when not playing boston, a card game he was very fond of, was that of listener, especially when he succeeded in setting two loquacious talkers at one another.

â€œWell, then, old chap, mon trÃ¨s honorable Alphonse KÃ¡rlovich,â€ said ShinshÃ­n, laughing ironically and mixing the most ordinary Russian expressions with the choicest French phrasesâ€”which was a peculiarity of his speech. â€œVous comptez vous faire des rentes sur lâ€™Ã©tat; * you want to make something out of your company?â€
* You expect to make an income out of the government.
â€œNo, Peter NikolÃ¡evich; I only want to show that in the cavalry the advantages are far less than in the infantry. Just consider my own position now, Peter NikolÃ¡evich...â€

Berg always spoke quietly, politely, and with great precision. His conversation always related entirely to himself; he would remain calm and silent when the talk related to any topic that had no direct bearing on himself. He could remain silent for hours without being at all put out of countenance himself or making others uncomfortable, but as soon as the conversation concerned himself he would begin to talk circumstantially and with evident satisfaction.

â€œConsider my position, Peter NikolÃ¡evich. Were I in the cavalry I should get not more than two hundred rubles every four months, even with the rank of lieutenant; but as it is I receive two hundred and thirty,â€ said he, looking at ShinshÃ­n and the count with a joyful, pleasant smile, as if it were obvious to him that his success must always be the chief desire of everyone else.

â€œBesides that, Peter NikolÃ¡evich, by exchanging into the Guards I shall be in a more prominent position,â€ continued Berg, â€œand vacancies occur much more frequently in the Foot Guards. Then just think what can be done with two hundred and thirty rubles! I even manage to put a little aside and to send something to my father,â€ he went on, emitting a smoke ring.
â€œLa balance y est... * A German knows how to skin a flint, as the proverb says,â€ remarked ShinshÃ­n, moving his pipe to the other side of his mouth and winking at the count.

payload.msg: The count burst out laughing. The other guests seeing that ShinshÃ­n was talking came up to listen. Berg, oblivious of irony or indifference, continued to explain how by exchanging into the Guards he had already gained a step on his old comrades of the Cadet Corps; how in wartime the company commander might get killed and he, as senior in the company, might easily succeed to the post; how popular he was with everyone in the regiment, and how satisfied his father was with him. Berg evidently enjoyed narrating all this, and did not seem to suspect that others, too, might have their own interests. But all he said was so prettily sedate, and the naÃ¯vetÃ© of his youthful egotism was so obvious, that he disarmed his hearers.
â€œWell, my boy, youâ€™ll get along wherever you goâ€”foot or horseâ€”that Iâ€™ll warrant,â€ said ShinshÃ­n, patting him on the shoulder and taking his feet off the sofa.
Berg smiled joyously. The count, followed by his guests, went into the drawing room.

It was just the moment before a big dinner when the assembled guests, expecting the summons to zakÃºska, * avoid engaging in any long conversation but think it necessary to move about and talk, in order to show that they are not at all impatient for their food. The host and hostess look toward the door, and now and then glance at one another, and the visitors try to guess from these glances who, or what, they are waiting forâ€”some important relation who has not yet arrived, or a dish that is not yet ready.

Pierre had come just at dinnertime and was sitting awkwardly in the middle of the drawing room on the first chair he had come across, blocking the way for everyone. The countess tried to make him talk, but he went on naÃ¯vely looking around through his spectacles as if in search of somebody and answered all her questions in monosyllables. He was in the way and was the only one who did not notice the fact. Most of the guests, knowing of the affair with the bear, looked with curiosity at this big, stout, quiet man, wondering how such a clumsy, modest fellow could have played such a prank on a policeman.
â€œYou have only lately arrived?â€ the countess asked him.
â€œOui, madame,â€ replied he, looking around him.
â€œYou have not yet seen my husband?â€
â€œNon, madame.â€ He smiled quite inappropriately.
â€œYou have been in Paris recently, I believe? I suppose itâ€™s very interesting.â€

The countess exchanged glances with Anna MikhÃ¡ylovna. The latter understood that she was being asked to entertain this young man, and sitting down beside him she began to speak about his father; but he answered her, as he had the countess, only in monosyllables. The other guests were all conversing with one another. â€œThe RazumÃ³vskis... It was charming... You are very kind... Countess AprÃ¡ksina...â€ was heard on all sides. The countess rose and went into the ballroom.
â€œMÃ¡rya DmÃ­trievna?â€ came her voice from there.
â€œHerself,â€ came the answer in a rough voice, and MÃ¡rya DmÃ­trievna entered the room.

All the unmarried ladies and even the married ones except the very oldest rose. MÃ¡rya DmÃ­trievna paused at the door. Tall and stout, holding high her fifty-year-old head with its gray curls, she stood surveying the guests, and leisurely arranged her wide sleeves as if rolling them up. MÃ¡rya DmÃ­trievna always spoke in Russian.

â€œHealth and happiness to her whose name day we are keeping and to her children,â€ she said, in her loud, full-toned voice which drowned all others. â€œWell, you old sinner,â€ she went on, turning to the count who was kissing her hand, â€œyouâ€™re feeling dull in Moscow, I daresay? Nowhere to hunt with your dogs? But what is to be done, old man? Just see how these nestlings are growing up,â€ and she pointed to the girls. â€œYou must look for husbands for them whether you like it or not....â€

â€œWell,â€ said she, â€œhowâ€™s my Cossack?â€ (MÃ¡rya DmÃ­trievna always called NatÃ¡sha a Cossack) and she stroked the childâ€™s arm as she came up fearless and gay to kiss her hand. â€œI know sheâ€™s a scamp of a girl, but I like her.â€

She took a pair of pear-shaped ruby earrings from her huge reticule and, having given them to the rosy NatÃ¡sha, who beamed with the pleasure of her saintâ€™s-day fete, turned away at once and addressed herself to Pierre.

â€œEh, eh, friend! Come here a bit,â€ said she, assuming a soft high tone of voice. â€œCome here, my friend...â€ and she ominously tucked up her sleeves still higher. Pierre approached, looking at her in a childlike way through his spectacles.

â€œCome nearer, come nearer, friend! I used to be the only one to tell your father the truth when he was in favor, and in your case itâ€™s my evident duty.â€ She paused. All were silent, expectant of what was to follow, for this was clearly only a prelude.

â€œA fine lad! My word! A fine lad!... His father lies on his deathbed and he amuses himself setting a policeman astride a bear! For shame, sir, for shame! It would be better if you went to the war.â€
She turned away and gave her hand to the count, who could hardly keep from laughing.
â€œWell, I suppose it is time we were at table?â€ said MÃ¡rya DmÃ­trievna.

The count went in first with MÃ¡rya DmÃ­trievna, the countess followed on the arm of a colonel of hussars, a man of importance to them because Nicholas was to go with him to the regiment; then came Anna MikhÃ¡ylovna with ShinshÃ­n. Berg gave his arm to VÃ©ra. The smiling Julie KarÃ¡gina went in with Nicholas. After them other couples followed, filling the whole dining hall, and last of all the children, tutors, and governesses followed singly. The footmen began moving about, chairs scraped, the band struck up in the gallery, and the guests settled down in their places. Then the strains of the countâ€™s household band were replaced by the clatter of knives and forks, the voices of visitors, and the soft steps of the footmen. At one end of the table sat the countess with MÃ¡rya DmÃ­trievna on her right and Anna MikhÃ¡ylovna on her left, the other lady visitors were farther down. At the other end sat the count, with the hussar colonel on his left and ShinshÃ­n and the other male visitors on his right. Midway down the long table on one side sat the grown-up young people: VÃ©ra beside Berg, and Pierre beside BorÃ­s; and on the other side, the children, tutors, and governesses. From behind the crystal decanters and fruit vases, the count kept glancing at his wife and her tall cap with its light-blue ribbons, and busily filled his neighborsâ€™ glasses, not neglecting his own. The countess in turn, without omitting her duties as hostess, threw significant glances from behind the pineapples at her husband whose face and bald head seemed by their redness to contrast more than usual with his gray hair. At the ladiesâ€™ end an even chatter of voices was heard all the time, at the menâ€™s end the voices sounded louder and louder, especially that of the colonel of hussars who, growing more and more flushed, ate and drank so much that the count held him up as a pattern to the other guests. Berg with tender smiles was saying to VÃ©ra that love is not an earthly but a heavenly feeling. BorÃ­s was telling his new friend Pierre who the guests were and exchanging glances with NatÃ¡sha, who was sitting opposite. Pierre spoke little but examined the new faces, and ate a great deal. Of the two soups he chose turtle with savory patties and went on to the game without omitting a single dish or one of the wines. These latter the butler thrust mysteriously forward, wrapped in a napkin, from behind the next manâ€™s shoulders and whispered: â€œDry Madeiraâ€... â€œHungarianâ€... or â€œRhine wineâ€ as the case might be. Of the four crystal glasses engraved with the countâ€™s monogram that stood before his plate, Pierre held out one at random and drank with enjoyment, gazing with ever-increasing amiability at the other guests. NatÃ¡sha, who sat opposite, was looking at BorÃ­s as girls of thirteen look at the boy they are in love with and have just kissed for the first time. Sometimes that same look fell on Pierre, and that funny lively little girlâ€™s look made him inclined to laugh without knowing why.

Nicholas sat at some distance from SÃ³nya, beside Julie KarÃ¡gina, to whom he was again talking with the same involuntary smile. SÃ³nya wore a company smile but was evidently tormented by jealousy; now she turned pale, now blushed and strained every nerve to overhear what Nicholas and Julie were saying to one another. The governess kept looking round uneasily as if preparing to resent any slight that might be put upon the children. The German tutor was trying to remember all the dishes, wines, and kinds of dessert, in order to send a full description of the dinner to his people in Germany; and he felt greatly offended when the butler with a bottle wrapped in a napkin passed him by. He frowned, trying to appear as if he did not want any of that wine, but was mortified because no one would understand that it was not to quench his thirst or from greediness that he wanted it, but simply from a conscientious desire for knowledge.

At the menâ€™s end of the table the talk grew more and more animated. The colonel told them that the declaration of war had already appeared in Petersburg and that a copy, which he had himself seen, had that day been forwarded by courier to the commander in chief.
â€œAnd why the deuce are we going to fight Bonaparte?â€ remarked ShinshÃ­n. â€œHe has stopped Austriaâ€™s cackle and I fear it will be our turn next.â€
The colonel was a stout, tall, plethoric German, evidently devoted to the service and patriotically Russian. He resented ShinshÃ­nâ€™s remark.

â€œIt is for the reasson, my goot sir,â€ said he, speaking with a German accent, â€œfor the reasson zat ze Emperor knows zat. He declares in ze manifessto zat he cannot fiew wiz indifference ze danger vreatening Russia and zat ze safety and dignity of ze Empire as vell as ze sanctity of its alliances...â€ he spoke this last word with particular emphasis as if in it lay the gist of the matter.
Then with the unerring official memory that characterized him he repeated from the opening words of the manifesto:

... and the wish, which constitutes the Emperorâ€™s sole and absolute aimâ€”to establish peace in Europe on firm foundationsâ€”has now decided him to despatch part of the army abroad and to create a new condition for the attainment of that purpose.
â€œZat, my dear sir, is vy...â€ he concluded, drinking a tumbler of wine with dignity and looking to the count for approval.

â€œConnaissez-vous le Proverbe:* â€˜Jerome, Jerome, do not roam, but turn spindles at home!â€™?â€ said ShinshÃ­n, puckering his brows and smiling. â€œCela nous convient Ã  merveille.*(2) SuvÃ³rov nowâ€”he knew what he was about; yet they beat him Ã  plate couture,*(3) and where are we to find SuvÃ³rovs now? Je vous demande un peu,â€ *(4) said he, continually changing from French to Russian.
*(2) That suits us down to the ground.

â€œVe must vight to the last tr-r-op of our plood!â€ said the colonel, thumping the table; â€œand ve must tie for our Emperor, and zen all vill pe vell. And ve must discuss it as little as po-o-ossibleâ€... he dwelt particularly on the word possible... â€œas po-o-ossible,â€ he ended, again turning to the count. â€œZat is how ve old hussars look at it, and zereâ€™s an end of it! And how do you, a young man and a young hussar, how do you judge of it?â€ he added, addressing Nicholas, who when he heard that the war was being discussed had turned from his partner with eyes and ears intent on the colonel.

â€œI am quite of your opinion,â€ replied Nicholas, flaming up, turning his plate round and moving his wineglasses about with as much decision and desperation as though he were at that moment facing some great danger. â€œI am convinced that we Russians must die or conquer,â€ he concluded, consciousâ€”as were othersâ€”after the words were uttered that his remarks were too enthusiastic and emphatic for the occasion and were therefore awkward.
â€œWhat you said just now was splendid!â€ said his partner Julie.
SÃ³nya trembled all over and blushed to her ears and behind them and down to her neck and shoulders while Nicholas was speaking.

Donâ€™t upset yourself, and let us talk sensibly while there is still time, be it a day or be it but an hour.... Tell me all you know about the will, and above all where it is. You must know. We will take it at once and show it to the count. He has, no doubt, forgotten it and will wish to destroy it. You understand that my sole desire is conscientiously to carry out his wishes; that is my only reason for being here. I came simply to help him and you.â€
